question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: FINAL ANSWER: Summary - Short answer: This matches a known Direct‑Routing failure where a newly provisioned carrier number block was not present in the SBC translation/normalization rules. The carrier confirmed delivery to your SIP trunk/SBC but the SBC answered INVITEs for the new range with 404 (so Teams sees nothing). A temporary SBC translation rule restored calls in tests. Based on this, classify the issue as 2nd‑level (Unified Communications / SBC) work, not a pure 1st‑level helpdesk ticket. This conclusion is based on the incident record and remediation pattern in your tickets
context: SOURCE 8: ﻿ Ticket summary: Ticket name: KF52: Microsoft Teams – Incoming calls from specific numbers not received Ticket number: TI-1612036 Company: Customer Company 52 AG Contact person: Contact Person 22 Owner: Employee 41 Origin: Email Type: Incident Created on: 18.09.25 08:56 Closure Closure date: 29.09.25 16:18 Closure text: Analysis completed. Routing and translation rules adjusted according to Direct Routing Blueprint. Incoming calls fully functional again. Activities 18.09.25 08:40 – Email from Contact Person 22 Good morning We have noticed that incoming calls from certain customers are no longer reaching us. Callers report that they either receive a busy tone or the connection is disconnected after a few seconds. Other external calls are functioning normally. Could you please check this? Kind regards 18.09.25 08:56 Ticket created and assigned to Employee 41. 18.09.25 09:25 – Helpdesk inquiry Good day Could you please provide the following information: – Example phone numbers where the issue occurs – Since when the issue has existed – Whether all users are affected or only specific ones Thank you. 18.09.25 10:15 – Customer response Here are two examples: +41 44 785 23 10 +41 44 785 23 11 Other numbers such as +41 31 … work without issue. The problem has existed since last week. All employees are affected. 18.09.25 11:40 – Internal analysis Test call from private mobile (different number range) → works. Test with number in specified range → call not delivered. In the Teams Admin Center, no incoming call is shown. 19.09.25 09:05 – Initial hypothesis Possible provider issue. Carrier contacted. 19.09.25 14:20 – Carrier feedback From the carrier’s perspective, the call is correctly delivered to the company’s SBC. No routing errors visible on their side. 22.09.25 08:55 – SBC log analysis For functioning numbers: INVITE accepted and forwarded to Teams. For affected numbers: INVITE received but immediately answered with “404 Not Found”. 22.09.25 11:30 – Email to customer Good day We see that incoming calls from the provider are reaching the SBC. However, the requests are not being correctly forwarded internally. Were there recent changes to number ranges or new extensions? Kind regards 22.09.25 13:05 – Customer response Yes, we received a new number range from the provider last week and partially migrated old numbers. The affected numbers belong to this new range. 23.09.25 09:10 – Internal review Comparison with document “Blueprint – Microsoft Teams Direct Routing & Number Translation – Version 2.3” Section regarding: • Normalization of incoming numbers • E.164 format • Translation rules on SBC • Voice routing policies Finding: New numbers are not included in the SBC translation table. Routing rule covers only the old number range. 24.09.25 – Test Temporary adjustment of translation rule for new number range. Test call performed. Call correctly signaled and delivered. Reverting to original configuration → issue reproducible. 25.09.25 – Coordination with customer Confirmation that new number range was fully migrated. Internal documentation had not yet been updated. 26.09.25 – Adjustment Routing and translation rules extended according to architectural specification. Details not documented in ticket for security reasons. 29.09.25 – Customer feedback All tested numbers are working again. Multiple customers also confirmed successful calls. Thank you for the support. 29.09.25 16:18 – Ticket closure Dear Contact Person 22 The root cause was an inconsistent translation and routing configuration following the introduction of a new number range. The configuration was adjusted according to the internal Direct Routing architecture. Incoming calls are now correctly delivered again. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 74: # SBC Translation Table / Direct Routing — Missing New Number Range This community centers on the PSTN-to-Teams Direct Routing chain: the Carrier/Provider delivers calls to the customer's SBC, the SBC translation table implements the Routing and Translation Rules (including E.164 normalization), and those rules feed Voice Routing Policies that route calls to Teams. During a recent migration the carrier provisioned a New Number Range which was partially migrated by the customer; those new numbers (+41 44 785 23 10/11 are given as examples) were not present in the SBC translation table and associated routing rules, causing INVITEs for that range to be mis-answered (404) and not forwarded to Teams. The carrier confirmed upstream delivery to the SBC, helping isolate the failure to SBC-side translation/routing configuration rather than upstream provisioning. [Data: Entities (177,178,179,180,181,183), Relationships (274,276,278,279,282,275)] ## Primary technical root cause: missing new-range entries in the SBC translation table Investigation identified that new telephone numbers provisioned by the carrier were not present in the SBC translation table, so INVITEs for that range were mis-answered (404) and not forwarded into Teams. This indicates a configuration gap where the concrete SBC translation entries did not include the new number range(s) introduced during migration [Data: Entities (178,183), Relationships (276,282)]. Because the SBC translation table is the concrete implementation of the Routing and Translation Rules, an omission at that configuration artifact directly prevented normalization and mapping for the affected numbers, breaking the call delivery chain to Teams until the entries were added to the table. This is consistent with the relationships tying the translation table to routing rules and the old/new range contrast observed during troubleshooting [Data: Entities (178,179,183), Relationships (276,282)]. ## Carrier confirmation isolates failure to SBC-side processing (not upstream delivery) The carrier/provider reported that they delivered calls correctly to the SBC and saw no routing errors on their side, which helped rule out upstream delivery issues and focus the investigation on SBC processing and configuration [Data: Entities (177), Relationships (274)]. Operationally, this eliminates carrier routing as the immediate cause and shortens the troubleshooting path to SBC translation and normalization logic, which should be the primary focus for immediate remediation and validation steps. The existing carrier confirmation should be recorded in the incident timeline and retained as evidence for post-incident review and change verification [Data: Relationships (274)]. ## Dependency chain: Routing & Translation Rules → E.164 normalization → Voice Routing Policies The components form a linear dependency chain where Routing and Translation Rules implement E.164 normalization and feed normalized numbers into Voice Routing Policies that determine Teams delivery; failures within the translation layer therefore propagate downstream to voice routing and call delivery [Data: Relationships (278,279), Entities (179,181,180)]. This chain highlights a single-point-of-impact: while multiple components participate in call delivery, the translation table acts as the authoritative mapping layer. Any gaps or misconfigurations in the translation table or the translation rules will prevent normalized address generation and thus prevent voice routing policies from matching and delivering calls to Teams users or voicemail/auto-attendants [Data: Entities (178,179,180)]. ## Migration and change-control gap: new range was provisioned but translation rules were not updated The new number range was assigned/provisioned by the carrier and partially migrated by the customer the week prior to the issue, creating a requirement to update SBC translation rules that was not met prior to cutover; the relationship metadata explicitly links the migration to the need to update SBC entries [Data: Entities (183), Relationships (275)]. This sequence suggests a process gap between telecommunications provisioning and SBC configuration change (e.g., missing checklist item, absent automated provisioning, or an incomplete change request). That control gap materially increased the likelihood of service disruption for the new range and should be addressed by process and governance changes (see recommendations). ## Observed failure mode: INVITEs mis-answered with 404 and not forwarded to Teams The direct symptom reported was that INVITEs for numbers in the new range were mis-answered with 404 responses and were not forwarded into Teams, consistent with missing or mismatched normalization/mapping entries on the SBC that prevented routing policies from matching the calls to internal destinations [Data: Entities (178,183), Relationships (276,282)]. This failure mode is reproducible and actionable: adding the correct normalization and translation entries for the new range should return correct call handling and prevent 404 mis-answers for those numbers. As such, verification steps can be scripted into a runbook for validation after updates are applied. ## Immediate remediation priorities and validation steps The highest-priority corrective action is to add the new-number-range entries to the SBC translation table and ensure the corresponding routing/translation rules implement correct E.164 normalization so the Voice Routing Policies match the calls to Teams endpoints [Data: Entities (178,179,181), Relationships (276,279,278)]. Follow-up validation should include (a) inbound test calls from the carrier for affected sample numbers, (b) SBC logs verifying correct translation and normalization, (c) verification that calls reach Teams and that no 404s are returned, and (d) a change-control record that ties the carrier provisioning event to the SBC configuration change to close the loop. Retain the carrier’s confirmation of upstream delivery as supporting evidence [Data: Entities (177,183), Relationships (274,275)]. ## Operational risk and recurrence: process and monitoring deficiencies The root cause and migration timeline point to a repeatable operational risk: when carriers provision new ranges, failure to propagate those ranges into SBC translation rules will repeatedly cause call delivery failures. The presence of an existing correctly functioning old number range makes such omissions less obvious until customers or users on the new range report failures [Data: Entities (184,183), Relationships (282)]. Mitigations should include: a) formalized handoff checklist between carrier provisioning and SBC configuration owners, b) automated reconciliation that compares carrier-provided ranges to SBC translation table contents, c) monitoring/alerting for abnormal 404 rates on the SBC for inbound PSTN INVITEs, and d) test call validation as part of cutover acceptance. ## Stakeholder mapping and escalation pathway Key stakeholders in this community are the Carrier/Provider (responsible for PSTN provisioning and initial delivery), the SBC owner/telephony/networking team (responsible for translation tables and routing rules), and the Teams/UC platform team (consumer of normalized addresses via Voice Routing Policies). The relationships show direct handoffs from Carrier → SBC and SBC configuration → Voice Routing Policies that affect Teams delivery [Data: Entities (177,178,180,179), Relationships (274,278)]. Escalation should follow: (1) carrier confirms delivery (already done) to quickly isolate the issue, (2) SBC/telephony team applies translation updates and notifies Teams/UC team, (3) Teams/UC validates call delivery and closes the loop, and (4) change-control and post-incident review document the migration and corrective steps for governance and audit. REPORT 76: # Direct Routing / SBC Incident: TI-1612036 — Customer Company 52 AG This community centers on a production Microsoft Teams Direct Routing incident (TI-1612036) opened for Customer Company 52 AG where inbound calls from specific PSTN numbers failed to be delivered. Investigation by the Unified Communications / Network team and Employee 41 showed SIP signaling and SDP negotiation succeeded but RTP was one-way: Microsoft-originating RTP reached the SBC while client-originating RTP did not consistently reach it. The carrier confirmed delivery to the SIP trunk/SBC, and a temporary SBC translation/normalization rule that included the new number range produced successful test calls; when reverted, the problem returned. Remediation consisted of adjusting SBC translation/routing rules per the Direct Routing blueprint and validating the fix, then closing the ticket after successful tests. Key components engaged were the SBC, Teams Admin Center/PowerShell for diagnostics, the SIP trunk, the carrier, Employee 41 as ticket owner, and the Unified Communications / Network team as technical owner. [Data: Entities (171, 114, 175, 173, 186, 185, 440); Relationships (257, 178, 731, 732, 266, 267, 268)] ## Primary cause: SBC translation/normalization rules omitted the new number range, blocking inbound call delivery mapping The incident was reproducibly resolved by adding a translation/normalization rule that included the new number range; temporary rule tests returned successful calls and reverting the change reproduced the failure, indicating the missing/insufficient translation rule on the SBC was the primary delivery failure mode rather than transport or carrier delivery. This behavior is documented in the ticket narrative and the temporary translation rule entity. [Data: Entities (171, 186); Relationships (267, 266)] The carrier confirmed calls reached the SIP trunk and were delivered to the SBC, which helped exclude carrier-side transport as the root cause and focused the investigation on SBC-side handling (translation/routing) and mapping logic. That confirmation is captured in the SIP trunk and coordination relationship. [Data: Entities (185); Relationships (268)] ## Media-path anomaly (one-way audio) was observed and is distinct from the delivery problem SBC logs and analysis showed successful SIP signaling and SDP negotiation while RTP was asymmetric: Microsoft-originating RTP was visible to the SBC but client-originating RTP was not consistently seen, producing one-way audio for affected calls. These observations separate signaling success from media failures and were central to troubleshooting. [Data: Entities (114, 453, 440); Relationships (731, 732, 179)] The SBC description explicitly notes common causes for such media asymmetry (client-side NAT/firewall blocking, asymmetric routing, RTP pinholing issues, or media-anchoring misconfiguration), which frames follow-on checks for network path, NAT policies, and SBC media handling if one-way issues recur. These are documented diagnostic expectations from the SBC analysis. [Data: Entities (114)] ## Investigation workflow and stakeholders were clear: Employee 41 led triage and coordinated with carrier and Unified Communications team Employee 41 was the ticket owner who coordinated troubleshooting activities, performed SBC log review and tests, applied the temporary translation rule, and engaged the carrier regarding the SIP trunk. The record of Employee 41 making translation-table adjustments and coordinating with the carrier is explicit in the dataset. [Data: Entities (173); Relationships (266, 267, 268)] The Unified Communications / Network team performed SBC/media and firewall analysis as the technical escalation path for Direct Routing and media flow, confirming the organizational escalation and ownership model during this incident. This team’s involvement is documented in the relationships and entity entries. [Data: Entities (113); Relationships (178)] ## Observability: Teams Admin Center and PowerShell provided complementary diagnostics but showed gaps for the affected numbers Teams Admin Center was used to inspect incoming call activity, Direct Routing configuration, and SBC health; it showed no incoming call entries for the affected numbers, which helped narrow diagnostics. Teams PowerShell was used for deeper tenant-level configuration retrieval when needed. This combination was effective but highlights a visibility gap for specific inbound-number telemetry in the Admin Center UI. [Data: Entities (175); Relationships (734, 838)] Because the Admin Center did not show incoming call activity for those numbers, investigators relied on SBC logs and carrier confirmation—indicating that runbooks should prescribe parallel checks (SBC logs + carrier trace + Teams diagnostics) for inbound-number failures to avoid diagnostic blind spots. [Data: Entities (175, 114); Relationships (732, 268)] ## Scope and impact: customer-facing inbound telephony disruption for specific PSTN numbers The ticket was opened on behalf of Customer Company 52 AG and included example affected numbers provided by the contact person, establishing a scoped customer-impact incident (not a tenant-wide outage but affecting inbound reachability from a particular number range). The ticket timeline shows the incident was opened 18.09.25 and closed 29.09.25 after validation. [Data: Entities (171, 172); Relationships (257, 258)] Operational impact was high for the affected call flows (callers experienced busy/disconnect), requiring cross-team coordination and temporary configuration changes to validate the permanent fix, indicating this type of failure has direct customer-journey consequences even if limited to a subset of numbers. [Data: Entities (171)] ## Evidence chain supports corrective action but suggests need for permanent change control and verification A temporary translation rule was used successfully to validate that including the new number range corrected delivery, and permanent routing/translation adjustments were applied per blueprint before closing the ticket—this sequence (test with temporary rule, then permanent update) demonstrates sound validation practice. The incident records explicitly describe the temporary rule producing successful tests and being reverted to reproduce the problem prior to the permanent update. [Data: Entities (186, 171); Relationships (267, 266)] Recommendation: codify this validated test-and-promote pattern into a change control and runbook (temporary rule test -> carrier confirmation -> promote to permanent translation/routing change -> post-change monitoring) to reduce risk of regressions and provide audit trails for future similar issues. The data supports this lifecycle as effective in this case. [Data: Entities (186, 173); Relationships (266, 267)] ## Recurring risk vectors remain: NAT/firewall RTP pinholing and SBC media handling should be monitored Although translation rules were the confirmed delivery fault in this incident, the SBC analysis also called out client NAT/firewall blocking, asymmetric routing, or RTP pinholing as common causes of one-way media—meaning that even after rule changes, media-path vulnerabilities could manifest under different conditions. The SBC entity explicitly lists these potential causes. [Data: Entities (114)] Operational mitigations should include RTP path monitoring, NAT traversal health checks, firewall policy reviews (for pinned RTP port ranges), and SBC configuration audits to detect media-anchoring or symmetric-routing deviations before they impact customers. These measures align with the observed media anomalies in the SBC logs. [Data: Entities (114, 440); Relationships (731, 732)] ## Recommended governance and runbook updates to prevent recurrence and accelerate resolution Based on the incident evidence, prioritized changes include: (1) enforce a change-control step requiring test with temporary translation rule and carrier confirmation before permanent promotion; (2) add inbound-number-range discovery and translation-table validation to routine SBC configuration audits; (3) improve Teams Admin Center telemetry correlation for inbound number traces or document explicit dependency on SBC/carrier logs in runbooks; and (4) maintain an incident playbook assigning Employee 41/UC team roles for Direct Routing incidents. These recommendations follow directly from the observed investigation steps, tools used, and the successful remediation pattern. [Data: Entities (173, 113, 175, 186); Relationships (266, 267, 178, 734)] Additionally, include periodic drills that simulate missing translation entries and one-way RTP scenarios to verify monitoring, alerting, and cross-team coordination (carrier, SIP trunk owner, UC team) operate as expected. The incident’s reproducible test-and-revert sequence demonstrates that such drills are feasible and informative. [Data: Entities (186, 171); Relationships (267)]

[Data: Entities (172, 173); Relationships (258, 268)] ## Root cause localized to SBC translation/normalization rules, not transport Carrier-side delivery to the SIP trunk/SBC was confirmed as successful, which isolated the fault to SBC-side signaling/handling rather than transport or SIP trunk connectivity. [Data: Entities (185); Relationships (268)] A temporary translation/normalization rule that added the missing number range restored successful call delivery during tests, demonstrating that missing or incorrect translation on the SBC was the causal factor rather than upstream routing. This temporary rule produced successful test calls when active and the issue was reproducible when it was reverted. [Data: Entities (186); Relationships (267)] ## Reproducibility and diagnostic validation via temporary rule The investigation included applying a temporary translation rule to the SBC which corrected signaling and call delivery for the impacted numbers; the behavior was reproducible (successful when the rule was applied, failing when reverted), providing a clear, testable root-cause validation. This gives a strong evidence chain from hypothesis to remediation. [Data: Entities (186); Relationships (267)] Employee 41 executed/oversaw those diagnostic changes and coordinated tests with the carrier and customer, confirming both the test outcomes and the localization of the problem to SBC translation behavior. [Data: Entities (173); Relationships (267, 268)] ## Stakeholder and responsibility map is clear for this incident Customer Company 52 AG is the affected organization and Contact Person 22 is the reporting point-of-contact who provided diagnostic data (sample numbers and timeline). The ticket explicitly ties the incident to the customer. [Data: Entities (171, 172); Relationships (257, 258)] Employee 41 acted as the internal owner who performed analysis, coordinated with the carrier regarding the SIP trunk and SBC behavior, and implemented the temporary rule and final adjustments per the architecture blueprint. The carrier (external) is implicated as the SIP trunk provider who delivered calls to the SBC but did not require changes to transport. [Data: Entities (173, 185); Relationships (268)] ## Alignment with Direct Routing blueprint and change artifacts The permanent adjustments were applied 'per Direct Routing Blueprint' and the ticket notes that routing and translation rules were adjusted in line with the architecture, indicating that the corrective change was made according to an existing design standard rather than an ad-hoc workaround. This suggests the organization has a blueprint guiding SBC translation/routing behavior for Direct Routing. [Data: Entities (171)] The use of a temporary translation rule during testing — followed by a documented permanent update — provides an audit trail and evidence that the change pathway (test -> validate -> apply permanent configuration) was followed for this incident. [Data: Entities (186, 171); Relationships (267)] ## Operational lessons and recommended controls (grounded to observed evidence) The reproducibility of the fault when the temporary rule was reverted demonstrates a deterministic failure mode tied to SBC translation rules; this supports implementing a formal change control patch/update for SBC translation tables to ensure all expected number ranges are present before service is placed into production. Evidence: temporary rule tests and restoration behavior. [Data: Entities (186); Relationships (267)] Because the carrier confirmed delivery to the SIP trunk/SBC, monitoring and alerting should include validation points beyond transport reachability—specifically, normalization/translation rule coverage and test call results for representative number ranges—so that future gaps are detected earlier. The ticket's test evidence and carrier/SBC confirmations provide the basis for these runbook and monitoring updates. [Data: Entities (185, 186, 171); Relationships (268, 267)]

The carrier confirmed delivery to the SIP trunk/SBC, and a temporary SBC translation/normalization rule that included the new number range produced successful test calls; when reverted, the problem returned. Remediation consisted of adjusting SBC translation/routing rules per the Direct Routing blueprint and validating the fix, then closing the ticket after successful tests. [Data: Entities (171, 114, 175, 173, 186, 185, 440); Relationships (257, 178, 731, 732, 266, 267, 268)] ## Primary cause: SBC translation/normalization rules omitted the new number range, blocking inbound call delivery mapping The incident was reproducibly resolved by adding a translation/normalization rule that included the new number range; temporary rule tests returned successful calls and reverting the change reproduced the failure, indicating the missing/insufficient translation rule on the SBC was the primary delivery failure mode rather than transport or carrier delivery. This behavior is documented in the ticket narrative and the temporary translation rule entity. [Data: Entities (171, 186); Relationships (267, 266)] The carrier confirmed calls reached the SIP trunk and were delivered to the SBC, which helped exclude carrier-side transport as the root cause and focused the investigation on SBC-side handling (translation/routing) and mapping logic. [Data: Entities (175); Relationships (734, 838)] Because the Admin Center did not show incoming call activity for those numbers, investigators relied on SBC logs and carrier confirmation—indicating that runbooks should prescribe parallel checks (SBC logs + carrier trace + Teams diagnostics) for inbound-number failures to avoid diagnostic blind spots. [Data: Entities (175, 114); Relationships (732, 268)] ## Scope and impact: customer-facing inbound telephony disruption for specific PSTN numbers The ticket was opened on behalf of Customer Company 52 AG and included example affected numbers provided by the contact person, establishing a scoped customer-impact incident (not a tenant-wide outage but affecting inbound reachability from a particular number range). The ticket timeline shows the incident was opened 18.09.25 and closed 29.09.25 after validation. [Data: Entities (171, 172); Relationships (257, 258)] Operational impact was high for the affected call flows (callers experienced busy/disconnect), requiring cross-team coordination and temporary configuration changes to validate the permanent fix, indicating this type of failure has direct customer-journey consequences even if limited to a subset of numbers. [Data: Entities (171)] ## Evidence chain supports corrective action but suggests need for permanent change control and verification A temporary translation rule was used successfully to validate that including the new number range corrected delivery, and permanent routing/translation adjustments were applied per blueprint before closing the ticket—this sequence (test with temporary rule, then permanent update) demonstrates sound validation practice. [Data: Entities (186, 171); Relationships (267, 266)] Recommendation: codify this validated test-and-promote pattern into a change control and runbook (temporary rule test -> carrier confirmation -> promote to permanent translation/routing change -> post-change monitoring) to reduce risk of regressions and provide audit trails for future similar issues. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 74: # SBC Translation Table / Direct Routing — Missing New Number Range This community centers on the PSTN-to-Teams Direct Routing chain: the Carrier/Provider delivers calls to the customer's SBC, the SBC translation table implements the Routing and Translation Rules (including E.164 normalization), and those rules feed Voice Routing Policies that route calls to Teams. During a recent migration the carrier provisioned a New Number Range which was partially migrated by the customer; those new numbers (+41 44 785 23 10/11 are given as examples) were not present in the SBC translation table and associated routing rules, causing INVITEs for that range to be mis-answered (404) and not forwarded to Teams. The carrier confirmed upstream delivery to the SBC, helping isolate the failure to SBC-side translation/routing configuration rather than upstream provisioning. [Data: Entities (177,178,179,180,181,183), Relationships (274,276,278,279,282,275)] ## Primary technical root cause: missing new-range entries in the SBC translation table Investigation identified that new telephone numbers provisioned by the carrier were not present in the SBC translation table, so INVITEs for that range were mis-answered (404) and not forwarded into Teams. This indicates a configuration gap where the concrete SBC translation entries did not include the new number range(s) introduced during migration [Data: Entities (178,183), Relationships (276,282)]. Because the SBC translation table is the concrete implementation of the Routing and Translation Rules, an omission at that configuration artifact directly prevented normalization and mapping for the affected numbers, breaking the call delivery chain to Teams until the entries were added to the table. This is consistent with the relationships tying the translation table to routing rules and the old/new range contrast observed during troubleshooting [Data: Entities (178,179,183), Relationships (276,282)]. ## Carrier confirmation isolates failure to SBC-side processing (not upstream delivery) The carrier/provider reported that they delivered calls correctly to the SBC and saw no routing errors on their side, which helped rule out upstream delivery issues and focus the investigation on SBC processing and configuration [Data: Entities (177), Relationships (274)]. Operationally, this eliminates carrier routing as the immediate cause and shortens the troubleshooting path to SBC translation and normalization logic, which should be the primary focus for immediate remediation and validation steps. The existing carrier confirmation should be recorded in the incident timeline and retained as evidence for post-incident review and change verification [Data: Relationships (274)]. ## Dependency chain: Routing & Translation Rules → E.164 normalization → Voice Routing Policies The components form a linear dependency chain where Routing and Translation Rules implement E.164 normalization and feed normalized numbers into Voice Routing Policies that determine Teams delivery; failures within the translation layer therefore propagate downstream to voice routing and call delivery [Data: Relationships (278,279), Entities (179,181,180)]. This chain highlights a single-point-of-impact: while multiple components participate in call delivery, the translation table acts as the authoritative mapping layer. Any gaps or misconfigurations in the translation table or the translation rules will prevent normalized address generation and thus prevent voice routing policies from matching and delivering calls to Teams users or voicemail/auto-attendants [Data: Entities (178,179,180)]. ## Migration and change-control gap: new range was provisioned but translation rules were not updated The new number range was assigned/provisioned by the carrier and partially migrated by the customer the week prior to the issue, creating a requirement to update SBC translation rules that was not met prior to cutover; the relationship metadata explicitly links the migration to the need to update SBC entries [Data: Entities (183), Relationships (275)]. This sequence suggests a process gap between telecommunications provisioning and SBC configuration change (e.g., missing checklist item, absent automated provisioning, or an incomplete change request). That control gap materially increased the likelihood of service disruption for the new range and should be addressed by process and governance changes (see recommendations). ## Observed failure mode: INVITEs mis-answered with 404 and not forwarded to Teams The direct symptom reported was that INVITEs for numbers in the new range were mis-answered with 404 responses and were not forwarded into Teams, consistent with missing or mismatched normalization/mapping entries on the SBC that prevented routing policies from matching the calls to internal destinations [Data: Entities (178,183), Relationships (276,282)]. This failure mode is reproducible and actionable: adding the correct normalization and translation entries for the new range should return correct call handling and prevent 404 mis-answers for those numbers. As such, verification steps can be scripted into a runbook for validation after updates are applied. ## Immediate remediation priorities and validation steps The highest-priority corrective action is to add the new-number-range entries to the SBC translation table and ensure the corresponding routing/translation rules implement correct E.164 normalization so the Voice Routing Policies match the calls to Teams endpoints [Data: Entities (178,179,181), Relationships (276,279,278)]. Follow-up validation should include (a) inbound test calls from the carrier for affected sample numbers, (b) SBC logs verifying correct translation and normalization, (c) verification that calls reach Teams and that no 404s are returned, and (d) a change-control record that ties the carrier provisioning event to the SBC configuration change to close the loop. Retain the carrier’s confirmation of upstream delivery as supporting evidence [Data: Entities (177,183), Relationships (274,275)]. ## Operational risk and recurrence: process and monitoring deficiencies The root cause and migration timeline point to a repeatable operational risk: when carriers provision new ranges, failure to propagate those ranges into SBC translation rules will repeatedly cause call delivery failures. The presence of an existing correctly functioning old number range makes such omissions less obvious until customers or users on the new range report failures [Data: Entities (184,183), Relationships (282)]. Mitigations should include: a) formalized handoff checklist between carrier provisioning and SBC configuration owners, b) automated reconciliation that compares carrier-provided ranges to SBC translation table contents, c) monitoring/alerting for abnormal 404 rates on the SBC for inbound PSTN INVITEs, and d) test call validation as part of cutover acceptance. ## Stakeholder mapping and escalation pathway Key stakeholders in this community are the Carrier/Provider (responsible for PSTN provisioning and initial delivery), the SBC owner/telephony/networking team (responsible for translation tables and routing rules), and the Teams/UC platform team (consumer of normalized addresses via Voice Routing Policies). The relationships show direct handoffs from Carrier → SBC and SBC configuration → Voice Routing Policies that affect Teams delivery [Data: Entities (177,178,180,179), Relationships (274,278)]. Escalation should follow: (1) carrier confirms delivery (already done) to quickly isolate the issue, (2) SBC/telephony team applies translation updates and notifies Teams/UC team, (3) Teams/UC validates call delivery and closes the loop, and (4) change-control and post-incident review document the migration and corrective steps for governance and audit.

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 76: # Direct Routing / SBC Incident: TI-1612036 — Customer Company 52 AG This community centers on a production Microsoft Teams Direct Routing incident (TI-1612036) opened for Customer Company 52 AG where inbound calls from specific PSTN numbers failed to be delivered. Investigation by the Unified Communications / Network team and Employee 41 showed SIP signaling and SDP negotiation succeeded but RTP was one-way: Microsoft-originating RTP reached the SBC while client-originating RTP did not consistently reach it. The carrier confirmed delivery to the SIP trunk/SBC, and a temporary SBC translation/normalization rule that included the new number range produced successful test calls; when reverted, the problem returned. Remediation consisted of adjusting SBC translation/routing rules per the Direct Routing blueprint and validating the fix, then closing the ticket after successful tests. Key components engaged were the SBC, Teams Admin Center/PowerShell for diagnostics, the SIP trunk, the carrier, Employee 41 as ticket owner, and the Unified Communications / Network team as technical owner. [Data: Entities (171, 114, 175, 173, 186, 185, 440); Relationships (257, 178, 731, 732, 266, 267, 268)] ## Primary cause: SBC translation/normalization rules omitted the new number range, blocking inbound call delivery mapping The incident was reproducibly resolved by adding a translation/normalization rule that included the new number range; temporary rule tests returned successful calls and reverting the change reproduced the failure, indicating the missing/insufficient translation rule on the SBC was the primary delivery failure mode rather than transport or carrier delivery. This behavior is documented in the ticket narrative and the temporary translation rule entity. [Data: Entities (171, 186); Relationships (267, 266)] The carrier confirmed calls reached the SIP trunk and were delivered to the SBC, which helped exclude carrier-side transport as the root cause and focused the investigation on SBC-side handling (translation/routing) and mapping logic. That confirmation is captured in the SIP trunk and coordination relationship. [Data: Entities (185); Relationships (268)] ## Media-path anomaly (one-way audio) was observed and is distinct from the delivery problem SBC logs and analysis showed successful SIP signaling and SDP negotiation while RTP was asymmetric: Microsoft-originating RTP was visible to the SBC but client-originating RTP was not consistently seen, producing one-way audio for affected calls. These observations separate signaling success from media failures and were central to troubleshooting. [Data: Entities (114, 453, 440); Relationships (731, 732, 179)] The SBC description explicitly notes common causes for such media asymmetry (client-side NAT/firewall blocking, asymmetric routing, RTP pinholing issues, or media-anchoring misconfiguration), which frames follow-on checks for network path, NAT policies, and SBC media handling if one-way issues recur. These are documented diagnostic expectations from the SBC analysis. [Data: Entities (114)] ## Investigation workflow and stakeholders were clear: Employee 41 led triage and coordinated with carrier and Unified Communications team Employee 41 was the ticket owner who coordinated troubleshooting activities, performed SBC log review and tests, applied the temporary translation rule, and engaged the carrier regarding the SIP trunk. Activities 18.09.25 08:40 – Email from Contact Person 22 Good morning We have noticed that incoming calls from certain customers are no longer reaching us. Kind regards 18.09.25 08:56 Ticket created and assigned to Employee 41. 18.09.25 09:25 – Helpdesk inquiry Good day Could you please provide the following information: – Example phone numbers where the issue occurs – Since when the issue has existed – Whether all users are affected or only specific ones Thank you. 18.09.25 10:15 – Customer response Here are two examples: +41 44 785 23 10 +41 44 785 23 11 Other numbers such as +41 31 … work without issue. The problem has existed since last week. All employees are affected. 18.09.25 11:40 – Internal analysis Test call from private mobile (different number range) → works. Test with number in specified range → call not delivered. In the Teams Admin Center, no incoming call is shown. 19.09.25 09:05 – Initial hypothesis Possible provider issue. However, the requests are not being correctly forwarded internally. Were there recent changes to number ranges or new extensions? Kind regards 22.09.25 13:05 – Customer response Yes, we received a new number range from the provider last week and partially migrated old numbers. The affected numbers belong to this new range. 23.09.25 09:10 – Internal review Comparison with document “Blueprint – Microsoft Teams Direct Routing & Number Translation – Version 2.3” Section regarding: • Normalization of incoming numbers • E.164 format • Translation rules on SBC • Voice routing policies Finding: New numbers are not included in the SBC translation table. Routing rule covers only the old number range. 24.09.25 – Test Temporary adjustment of translation rule for new number range. Test call performed. Call correctly signaled and delivered. Reverting to original configuration → issue reproducible. 25.09.25 – Coordination with customer Confirmation that new number range was fully migrated. Internal documentation had not yet been updated. 26.09.25 – Adjustment Routing and translation rules extended according to architectural specification. Details not documented in ticket for security reasons. 29.09.25 – Customer feedback All tested numbers are working again. Multiple customers also confirmed successful calls. Thank you for the support. 29.09.25 16:18 – Ticket closure Dear Contact Person 22 The root cause was an inconsistent translation and routing configuration following the introduction of a new number range. The configuration was adjusted according to the internal Direct Routing architecture. Incoming calls are now correctly delivered again. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 74: # SBC Translation Table / Direct Routing — Missing New Number Range This community centers on the PSTN-to-Teams Direct Routing chain: the Carrier/Provider delivers calls to the customer's SBC, the SBC translation table implements the Routing and Translation Rules (including E.164 normalization), and those rules feed Voice Routing Policies that route calls to Teams. During a recent migration the carrier provisioned a New Number Range which was partially migrated by the customer; those new numbers (+41 44 785 23 10/11 are given as examples) were not present in the SBC translation table and associated routing rules, causing INVITEs for that range to be mis-answered (404) and not forwarded to Teams. The carrier confirmed upstream delivery to the SBC, helping isolate the failure to SBC-side translation/routing configuration rather than upstream provisioning. [Data: Entities (177,178,179,180,181,183), Relationships (274,276,278,279,282,275)] ## Primary technical root cause: missing new-range entries in the SBC translation table Investigation identified that new telephone numbers provisioned by the carrier were not present in the SBC translation table, so INVITEs for that range were mis-answered (404) and not forwarded into Teams. This indicates a configuration gap where the concrete SBC translation entries did not include the new number range(s) introduced during migration [Data: Entities (178,183), Relationships (276,282)]. Because the SBC translation table is the concrete implementation of the Routing and Translation Rules, an omission at that configuration artifact directly prevented normalization and mapping for the affected numbers, breaking the call delivery chain to Teams until the entries were added to the table. This is consistent with the relationships tying the translation table to routing rules and the old/new range contrast observed during troubleshooting [Data: Entities (178,179,183), Relationships (276,282)]. ## Carrier confirmation isolates failure to SBC-side processing (not upstream delivery) The carrier/provider reported that they delivered calls correctly to the SBC and saw no routing errors on their side, which helped rule out upstream delivery issues and focus the investigation on SBC processing and configuration [Data: Entities (177), Relationships (274)]. Operationally, this eliminates carrier routing as the immediate cause and shortens the troubleshooting path to SBC translation and normalization logic, which should be the primary focus for immediate remediation and validation steps. The existing carrier confirmation should be recorded in the incident timeline and retained as evidence for post-incident review and change verification [Data: Relationships (274)]. ## Dependency chain: Routing & Translation Rules → E.164 normalization → Voice Routing Policies The components form a linear dependency chain where Routing and Translation Rules implement E.164 normalization and feed normalized numbers into Voice Routing Policies that determine Teams delivery; failures within the translation layer therefore propagate downstream to voice routing and call delivery [Data: Relationships (278,279), Entities (179,181,180)]. This chain highlights a single-point-of-impact: while multiple components participate in call delivery, the translation table acts as the authoritative mapping layer. Any gaps or misconfigurations in the translation table or the translation rules will prevent normalized address generation and thus prevent voice routing policies from matching and delivering calls to Teams users or voicemail/auto-attendants [Data: Entities (178,179,180)]. ## Migration and change-control gap: new range was provisioned but translation rules were not updated The new number range was assigned/provisioned by the carrier and partially migrated by the customer the week prior to the issue, creating a requirement to update SBC translation rules that was not met prior to cutover; the relationship metadata explicitly links the migration to the need to update SBC entries [Data: Entities (183), Relationships (275)]. This sequence suggests a process gap between telecommunications provisioning and SBC configuration change (e.g., missing checklist item, absent automated provisioning, or an incomplete change request). That control gap materially increased the likelihood of service disruption for the new range and should be addressed by process and governance changes (see recommendations). ## Observed failure mode: INVITEs mis-answered with 404 and not forwarded to Teams The direct symptom reported was that INVITEs for numbers in the new range were mis-answered with 404 responses and were not forwarded into Teams, consistent with missing or mismatched normalization/mapping entries on the SBC that prevented routing policies from matching the calls to internal destinations [Data: Entities (178,183), Relationships (276,282)]. This failure mode is reproducible and actionable: adding the correct normalization and translation entries for the new range should return correct call handling and prevent 404 mis-answers for those numbers. As such, verification steps can be scripted into a runbook for validation after updates are applied. ## Immediate remediation priorities and validation steps The highest-priority corrective action is to add the new-number-range entries to the SBC translation table and ensure the corresponding routing/translation rules implement correct E.164 normalization so the Voice Routing Policies match the calls to Teams endpoints [Data: Entities (178,179,181), Relationships (276,279,278)]. Follow-up validation should include (a) inbound test calls from the carrier for affected sample numbers, (b) SBC logs verifying correct translation and normalization, (c) verification that calls reach Teams and that no 404s are returned, and (d) a change-control record that ties the carrier provisioning event to the SBC configuration change to close the loop. Retain the carrier’s confirmation of upstream delivery as supporting evidence [Data: Entities (177,183), Relationships (274,275)]. ## Operational risk and recurrence: process and monitoring deficiencies The root cause and migration timeline point to a repeatable operational risk: when carriers provision new ranges, failure to propagate those ranges into SBC translation rules will repeatedly cause call delivery failures. The presence of an existing correctly functioning old number range makes such omissions less obvious until customers or users on the new range report failures [Data: Entities (184,183), Relationships (282)]. Mitigations should include: a) formalized handoff checklist between carrier provisioning and SBC configuration owners, b) automated reconciliation that compares carrier-provided ranges to SBC translation table contents, c) monitoring/alerting for abnormal 404 rates on the SBC for inbound PSTN INVITEs, and d) test call validation as part of cutover acceptance. ## Stakeholder mapping and escalation pathway Key stakeholders in this community are the Carrier/Provider (responsible for PSTN provisioning and initial delivery), the SBC owner/telephony/networking team (responsible for translation tables and routing rules), and the Teams/UC platform team (consumer of normalized addresses via Voice Routing Policies). The relationships show direct handoffs from Carrier → SBC and SBC configuration → Voice Routing Policies that affect Teams delivery [Data: Entities (177,178,180,179), Relationships (274,278)]. Escalation should follow: (1) carrier confirms delivery (already done) to quickly isolate the issue, (2) SBC/telephony team applies translation updates and notifies Teams/UC team, (3) Teams/UC validates call delivery and closes the loop, and (4) change-control and post-incident review document the migration and corrective steps for governance and audit. context: SOURCE 16: ﻿ Ticket summary: Ticket name: KF52: Microsoft Teams – External calls disconnect after approx. 30 seconds Ticket number: TI-1604879 Company: Customer Company 52 AG Contact person: Contact Person 16 Owner: Employee 35 Origin: Email Type: Incident Created on: 22.07.25 09:14 Closure Closure date: 31.07.25 15:52 Closure text: Analysis completed. Adjustment made according to Direct Routing architecture. Calls stable. Activities 22.07.25 09:02 – Email from Contact Person 16 to Helpdesk Good morning Since yesterday, several employees report that external calls in Microsoft Teams disconnect after approximately 30 seconds. The call is established normally, both parties can hear each other, but the connection is terminated after a short time. Internal Teams calls are not affected. Could you please review this? Kind regards 22.07.25 09:14 Ticket created and assigned to Employee 35. 22.07.25 10:05 – Helpdesk inquiry Good day Thank you for your report. Could you please confirm: – Does it affect incoming and outgoing calls? – Does the disconnection always occur after approximately the same duration? – Are all locations affected or only a specific one? Thank you very much. 22.07.25 10:42 – Customer response It only affects the Basel location. Both incoming and outgoing calls. The disconnection occurs almost exactly after 30 seconds, sometimes slightly earlier. Home office users are not affected. 22.07.25 11:30 – Internal note Test performed with affected user. External call to mobile phone. Call disconnected after 29–31 seconds. No error message in the Teams client, simply “Call Ended.” 23.07.25 08:55 – Initial hypothesis Possible client error or unstable connection. Test with another device → same behavior. Test with LAN cable instead of docking station → no change. 23.07.25 11:10 – Email to customer Good day We are currently analyzing the Direct Routing logs. Signaling is established correctly. The disconnection does not appear to be triggered by the client. Were there any recent network changes at the Basel location? Kind regards 23.07.25 13:45 – Customer response On Friday, a new network segment for VoIP traffic was implemented. A separate VLAN for “Voice” was created. Could this be relevant? 24.07.25 09:05 – Internal analysis SBC logs reviewed: – SIP INVITE successful – 200 OK correct – Call established – After 30 seconds, BYE from SBC No obvious error code. 24.07.25 10:30 – Firewall analysis No blocking visible. It explicitly describes NDES roles, failure modes, and stepwise remediation actions, making it the single consolidated reference for technicians deploying or operating this capability. Because the blueprint bundles decision-tree checks, monitoring guidance, and remediation steps (IIS components, AD CS post-configuration, service account settings, CA/template selection), it should be treated as the authoritative runbook for first- and second-line responders and as the basis for formalizing escalation and change processes for certificate issuance services. [Data: Entities (359); Relationships (579,583,372)] ## Active Directory Certificate Services (AD CS) is a single, high-impact dependency for NDES AD CS provides Root and Issuing CA roles, certificate templates, and lifecycle management and is explicitly described as the backend that NDES depends on; AD CS post-configuration is required for NDES to operate. Failures or misconfigurations in AD CS (expired CA certs, wrong CA selection, misconfigured templates) are identified as causes of authentication failures, connection drops, or inability to issue device certificates. Because AD CS issues and lifecycle-management are upstream of NDES, any operational or security change to the CA hierarchy affects device authentication broadly; therefore AD CS must be treated as a critical service with dedicated monitoring, change control, and escalation ownership. [Data: Entities (361); Relationships (596,600)] ## Decision tree encodes operational checks and prescribes escalation to infrastructure specialists The 'DECISION TREE – NDES INSTALLATION & OPERATION' contains a structured troubleshooting flow (seven verification steps) covering reachability, NDES service status, IIS components, AD CS post-configuration, service account configuration, CA/template selection, and final issuance testing—providing a reproducible first-line diagnostic workflow for technicians. context: SOURCE 8: ﻿ Ticket summary: Ticket name: KF52: Microsoft Teams – Incoming calls from specific numbers not received Ticket number: TI-1612036 Company: Customer Company 52 AG Contact person: Contact Person 22 Owner: Employee 41 Origin: Email Type: Incident Created on: 18.09.25 08:56 Closure Closure date: 29.09.25 16:18 Closure text: Analysis completed. Routing and translation rules adjusted according to Direct Routing Blueprint. Incoming calls fully functional again. Activities 18.09.25 08:40 – Email from Contact Person 22 Good morning We have noticed that incoming calls from certain customers are no longer reaching us. Callers report that they either receive a busy tone or the connection is disconnected after a few seconds. Other external calls are functioning normally. Could you please check this? Kind regards 18.09.25 08:56 Ticket created and assigned to Employee 41. 18.09.25 09:25 – Helpdesk inquiry Good day Could you please provide the following information: – Example phone numbers where the issue occurs – Since when the issue has existed – Whether all users are affected or only specific ones Thank you. 18.09.25 10:15 – Customer response Here are two examples: +41 44 785 23 10 +41 44 785 23 11 Other numbers such as +41 31 … work without issue. The problem has existed since last week. All employees are affected. 18.09.25 11:40 – Internal analysis Test call from private mobile (different number range) → works. Test with number in specified range → call not delivered. In the Teams Admin Center, no incoming call is shown. 19.09.25 09:05 – Initial hypothesis Possible provider issue. Carrier contacted. 19.09.25 14:20 – Carrier feedback From the carrier’s perspective, the call is correctly delivered to the company’s SBC. No routing errors visible on their side. 22.09.25 08:55 – SBC log analysis For functioning numbers: INVITE accepted and forwarded to Teams. For affected numbers: INVITE received but immediately answered with “404 Not Found”. 22.09.25 11:30 – Email to customer Good day We see that incoming calls from the provider are reaching the SBC. However, the requests are not being correctly forwarded internally. Were there recent changes to number ranges or new extensions? Kind regards 22.09.25 13:05 – Customer response Yes, we received a new number range from the provider last week and partially migrated old numbers. The affected numbers belong to this new range. 23.09.25 09:10 – Internal review Comparison with document “Blueprint – Microsoft Teams Direct Routing & Number Translation – Version 2.3” Section regarding: • Normalization of incoming numbers • E.164 format • Translation rules on SBC • Voice routing policies Finding: New numbers are not included in the SBC translation table. Routing rule covers only the old number range. 24.09.25 – Test Temporary adjustment of translation rule for new number range. Test call performed. Call correctly signaled and delivered. Reverting to original configuration → issue reproducible. 25.09.25 – Coordination with customer Confirmation that new number range was fully migrated. Internal documentation had not yet been updated. 26.09.25 – Adjustment Routing and translation rules extended according to architectural specification. Details not documented in ticket for security reasons. 29.09.25 – Customer feedback All tested numbers are working again. Multiple customers also confirmed successful calls. Thank you for the support. 29.09.25 16:18 – Ticket closure Dear Contact Person 22 The root cause was an inconsistent translation and routing configuration following the introduction of a new number range. The configuration was adjusted according to the internal Direct Routing architecture. Incoming calls are now correctly delivered again. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 74: # SBC Translation Table / Direct Routing — Missing New Number Range This community centers on the PSTN-to-Teams Direct Routing chain: the Carrier/Provider delivers calls to the customer's SBC, the SBC translation table implements the Routing and Translation Rules (including E.164 normalization), and those rules feed Voice Routing Policies that route calls to Teams. During a recent migration the carrier provisioned a New Number Range which was partially migrated by the customer; those new numbers (+41 44 785 23 10/11 are given as examples) were not present in the SBC translation table and associated routing rules, causing INVITEs for that range to be mis-answered (404) and not forwarded to Teams. The carrier confirmed upstream delivery to the SBC, helping isolate the failure to SBC-side translation/routing configuration rather than upstream provisioning. [Data: Entities (177,178,179,180,181,183), Relationships (274,276,278,279,282,275)] ## Primary technical root cause: missing new-range entries in the SBC translation table Investigation identified that new telephone numbers provisioned by the carrier were not present in the SBC translation table, so INVITEs for that range were mis-answered (404) and not forwarded into Teams. This indicates a configuration gap where the concrete SBC translation entries did not include the new number range(s) introduced during migration [Data: Entities (178,183), Relationships (276,282)]. Because the SBC translation table is the concrete implementation of the Routing and Translation Rules, an omission at that configuration artifact directly prevented normalization and mapping for the affected numbers, breaking the call delivery chain to Teams until the entries were added to the table. This is consistent with the relationships tying the translation table to routing rules and the old/new range contrast observed during troubleshooting [Data: Entities (178,179,183), Relationships (276,282)]. ## Carrier confirmation isolates failure to SBC-side processing (not upstream delivery) The carrier/provider reported that they delivered calls correctly to the SBC and saw no routing errors on their side, which helped rule out upstream delivery issues and focus the investigation on SBC processing and configuration [Data: Entities (177), Relationships (274)]. Operationally, this eliminates carrier routing as the immediate cause and shortens the troubleshooting path to SBC translation and normalization logic, which should be the primary focus for immediate remediation and validation steps. The existing carrier confirmation should be recorded in the incident timeline and retained as evidence for post-incident review and change verification [Data: Relationships (274)]. ## Dependency chain: Routing & Translation Rules → E.164 normalization → Voice Routing Policies The components form a linear dependency chain where Routing and Translation Rules implement E.164 normalization and feed normalized numbers into Voice Routing Policies that determine Teams delivery; failures within the translation layer therefore propagate downstream to voice routing and call delivery [Data: Relationships (278,279), Entities (179,181,180)]. This chain highlights a single-point-of-impact: while multiple components participate in call delivery, the translation table acts as the authoritative mapping layer. Any gaps or misconfigurations in the translation table or the translation rules will prevent normalized address generation and thus prevent voice routing policies from matching and delivering calls to Teams users or voicemail/auto-attendants [Data: Entities (178,179,180)]. ## Migration and change-control gap: new range was provisioned but translation rules were not updated The new number range was assigned/provisioned by the carrier and partially migrated by the customer the week prior to the issue, creating a requirement to update SBC translation rules that was not met prior to cutover; the relationship metadata explicitly links the migration to the need to update SBC entries [Data: Entities (183), Relationships (275)]. This sequence suggests a process gap between telecommunications provisioning and SBC configuration change (e.g., missing checklist item, absent automated provisioning, or an incomplete change request). That control gap materially increased the likelihood of service disruption for the new range and should be addressed by process and governance changes (see recommendations). ## Observed failure mode: INVITEs mis-answered with 404 and not forwarded to Teams The direct symptom reported was that INVITEs for numbers in the new range were mis-answered with 404 responses and were not forwarded into Teams, consistent with missing or mismatched normalization/mapping entries on the SBC that prevented routing policies from matching the calls to internal destinations [Data: Entities (178,183), Relationships (276,282)]. This failure mode is reproducible and actionable: adding the correct normalization and translation entries for the new range should return correct call handling and prevent 404 mis-answers for those numbers. As such, verification steps can be scripted into a runbook for validation after updates are applied. ## Immediate remediation priorities and validation steps The highest-priority corrective action is to add the new-number-range entries to the SBC translation table and ensure the corresponding routing/translation rules implement correct E.164 normalization so the Voice Routing Policies match the calls to Teams endpoints [Data: Entities (178,179,181), Relationships (276,279,278)]. Follow-up validation should include (a) inbound test calls from the carrier for affected sample numbers, (b) SBC logs verifying correct translation and normalization, (c) verification that calls reach Teams and that no 404s are returned, and (d) a change-control record that ties the carrier provisioning event to the SBC configuration change to close the loop. Retain the carrier’s confirmation of upstream delivery as supporting evidence [Data: Entities (177,183), Relationships (274,275)]. ## Operational risk and recurrence: process and monitoring deficiencies The root cause and migration timeline point to a repeatable operational risk: when carriers provision new ranges, failure to propagate those ranges into SBC translation rules will repeatedly cause call delivery failures. The presence of an existing correctly functioning old number range makes such omissions less obvious until customers or users on the new range report failures [Data: Entities (184,183), Relationships (282)]. Mitigations should include: a) formalized handoff checklist between carrier provisioning and SBC configuration owners, b) automated reconciliation that compares carrier-provided ranges to SBC translation table contents, c) monitoring/alerting for abnormal 404 rates on the SBC for inbound PSTN INVITEs, and d) test call validation as part of cutover acceptance. ## Stakeholder mapping and escalation pathway Key stakeholders in this community are the Carrier/Provider (responsible for PSTN provisioning and initial delivery), the SBC owner/telephony/networking team (responsible for translation tables and routing rules), and the Teams/UC platform team (consumer of normalized addresses via Voice Routing Policies). The relationships show direct handoffs from Carrier → SBC and SBC configuration → Voice Routing Policies that affect Teams delivery [Data: Entities (177,178,180,179), Relationships (274,278)]. Escalation should follow: (1) carrier confirms delivery (already done) to quickly isolate the issue, (2) SBC/telephony team applies translation updates and notifies Teams/UC team, (3) Teams/UC validates call delivery and closes the loop, and (4) change-control and post-incident review document the migration and corrective steps for governance and audit.

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 76: # Direct Routing / SBC Incident: TI-1612036 — Customer Company 52 AG This community centers on a production Microsoft Teams Direct Routing incident (TI-1612036) opened for Customer Company 52 AG where inbound calls from specific PSTN numbers failed to be delivered. Investigation by the Unified Communications / Network team and Employee 41 showed SIP signaling and SDP negotiation succeeded but RTP was one-way: Microsoft-originating RTP reached the SBC while client-originating RTP did not consistently reach it. The carrier confirmed delivery to the SIP trunk/SBC, and a temporary SBC translation/normalization rule that included the new number range produced successful test calls; when reverted, the problem returned. Remediation consisted of adjusting SBC translation/routing rules per the Direct Routing blueprint and validating the fix, then closing the ticket after successful tests. Key components engaged were the SBC, Teams Admin Center/PowerShell for diagnostics, the SIP trunk, the carrier, Employee 41 as ticket owner, and the Unified Communications / Network team as technical owner. [Data: Entities (171, 114, 175, 173, 186, 185, 440); Relationships (257, 178, 731, 732, 266, 267, 268)] ## Primary cause: SBC translation/normalization rules omitted the new number range, blocking inbound call delivery mapping The incident was reproducibly resolved by adding a translation/normalization rule that included the new number range; temporary rule tests returned successful calls and reverting the change reproduced the failure, indicating the missing/insufficient translation rule on the SBC was the primary delivery failure mode rather than transport or carrier delivery. This behavior is documented in the ticket narrative and the temporary translation rule entity. [Data: Entities (171, 186); Relationships (267, 266)] The carrier confirmed calls reached the SIP trunk and were delivered to the SBC, which helped exclude carrier-side transport as the root cause and focused the investigation on SBC-side handling (translation/routing) and mapping logic. That confirmation is captured in the SIP trunk and coordination relationship. [Data: Entities (185); Relationships (268)] ## Media-path anomaly (one-way audio) was observed and is distinct from the delivery problem SBC logs and analysis showed successful SIP signaling and SDP negotiation while RTP was asymmetric: Microsoft-originating RTP was visible to the SBC but client-originating RTP was not consistently seen, producing one-way audio for affected calls. These observations separate signaling success from media failures and were central to troubleshooting. [Data: Entities (114, 453, 440); Relationships (731, 732, 179)] The SBC description explicitly notes common causes for such media asymmetry (client-side NAT/firewall blocking, asymmetric routing, RTP pinholing issues, or media-anchoring misconfiguration), which frames follow-on checks for network path, NAT policies, and SBC media handling if one-way issues recur. These are documented diagnostic expectations from the SBC analysis. [Data: Entities (114)] ## Investigation workflow and stakeholders were clear: Employee 41 led triage and coordinated with carrier and Unified Communications team Employee 41 was the ticket owner who coordinated troubleshooting activities, performed SBC log review and tests, applied the temporary translation rule, and engaged the carrier regarding the SIP trunk. The record of Employee 41 making translation-table adjustments and coordinating with the carrier is explicit in the dataset. [Data: Entities (173); Relationships (266, 267, 268)] The Unified Communications / Network team performed SBC/media and firewall analysis as the technical escalation path for Direct Routing and media flow, confirming the organizational escalation and ownership model during this incident. This team’s involvement is documented in the relationships and entity entries. [Data: Entities (113); Relationships (178)] ## Observability: Teams Admin Center and PowerShell provided complementary diagnostics but showed gaps for the affected numbers Teams Admin Center was used to inspect incoming call activity, Direct Routing configuration, and SBC health; it showed no incoming call entries for the affected numbers, which helped narrow diagnostics. Teams PowerShell was used for deeper tenant-level configuration retrieval when needed. This combination was effective but highlights a visibility gap for specific inbound-number telemetry in the Admin Center UI. [Data: Entities (175); Relationships (734, 838)] Because the Admin Center did not show incoming call activity for those numbers, investigators relied on SBC logs and carrier confirmation—indicating that runbooks should prescribe parallel checks (SBC logs + carrier trace + Teams diagnostics) for inbound-number failures to avoid diagnostic blind spots. [Data: Entities (175, 114); Relationships (732, 268)] ## Scope and impact: customer-facing inbound telephony disruption for specific PSTN numbers The ticket was opened on behalf of Customer Company 52 AG and included example affected numbers provided by the contact person, establishing a scoped customer-impact incident (not a tenant-wide outage but affecting inbound reachability from a particular number range). The ticket timeline shows the incident was opened 18.09.25 and closed 29.09.25 after validation. [Data: Entities (171, 172); Relationships (257, 258)] Operational impact was high for the affected call flows (callers experienced busy/disconnect), requiring cross-team coordination and temporary configuration changes to validate the permanent fix, indicating this type of failure has direct customer-journey consequences even if limited to a subset of numbers. [Data: Entities (171)] ## Evidence chain supports corrective action but suggests need for permanent change control and verification A temporary translation rule was used successfully to validate that including the new number range corrected delivery, and permanent routing/translation adjustments were applied per blueprint before closing the ticket—this sequence (test with temporary rule, then permanent update) demonstrates sound validation practice. The incident records explicitly describe the temporary rule producing successful tests and being reverted to reproduce the problem prior to the permanent update. [Data: Entities (186, 171); Relationships (267, 266)] Recommendation: codify this validated test-and-promote pattern into a change control and runbook (temporary rule test -> carrier confirmation -> promote to permanent translation/routing change -> post-change monitoring) to reduce risk of regressions and provide audit trails for future similar issues. The data supports this lifecycle as effective in this case. [Data: Entities (186, 173); Relationships (266, 267)] ## Recurring risk vectors remain: NAT/firewall RTP pinholing and SBC media handling should be monitored Although translation rules were the confirmed delivery fault in this incident, the SBC analysis also called out client NAT/firewall blocking, asymmetric routing, or RTP pinholing as common causes of one-way media—meaning that even after rule changes, media-path vulnerabilities could manifest under different conditions. The SBC entity explicitly lists these potential causes. [Data: Entities (114)] Operational mitigations should include RTP path monitoring, NAT traversal health checks, firewall policy reviews (for pinned RTP port ranges), and SBC configuration audits to detect media-anchoring or symmetric-routing deviations before they impact customers. These measures align with the observed media anomalies in the SBC logs. [Data: Entities (114, 440); Relationships (731, 732)] ## Recommended governance and runbook updates to prevent recurrence and accelerate resolution Based on the incident evidence, prioritized changes include: (1) enforce a change-control step requiring test with temporary translation rule and carrier confirmation before permanent promotion; (2) add inbound-number-range discovery and translation-table validation to routine SBC configuration audits; (3) improve Teams Admin Center telemetry correlation for inbound number traces or document explicit dependency on SBC/carrier logs in runbooks; and (4) maintain an incident playbook assigning Employee 41/UC team roles for Direct Routing incidents. These recommendations follow directly from the observed investigation steps, tools used, and the successful remediation pattern. [Data: Entities (173, 113, 175, 186); Relationships (266, 267, 178, 734)] Additionally, include periodic drills that simulate missing translation entries and one-way RTP scenarios to verify monitoring, alerting, and cross-team coordination (carrier, SIP trunk owner, UC team) operate as expected. The incident’s reproducible test-and-revert sequence demonstrates that such drills are feasible and informative. [Data: Entities (186, 171); Relationships (267)]

Activities 22.07.25 09:02 – Email from Contact Person 16 to Helpdesk Good morning Since yesterday, several employees report that external calls in Microsoft Teams disconnect after approximately 30 seconds. The call is established normally, both parties can hear each other, but the connection is terminated after a short time. Internal Teams calls are not affected. Could you please review this? Kind regards 22.07.25 09:14 Ticket created and assigned to Employee 35. 22.07.25 10:05 – Helpdesk inquiry Good day Thank you for your report. Could you please confirm: – Does it affect incoming and outgoing calls? – Does the disconnection always occur after approximately the same duration? – Are all locations affected or only a specific one? Thank you very much. 22.07.25 10:42 – Customer response It only affects the Basel location. Both incoming and outgoing calls. The disconnection occurs almost exactly after 30 seconds, sometimes slightly earlier. Home office users are not affected. 22.07.25 11:30 – Internal note Test performed with affected user. External call to mobile phone. Call disconnected after 29–31 seconds. No error message in the Teams client, simply “Call Ended.” 23.07.25 08:55 – Initial hypothesis Possible client error or unstable connection. Test with another device → same behavior. Test with LAN cable instead of docking station → no change. 23.07.25 11:10 – Email to customer Good day We are currently analyzing the Direct Routing logs. Signaling is established correctly. The disconnection does not appear to be triggered by the client. Were there any recent network changes at the Basel location? Kind regards 23.07.25 13:45 – Customer response On Friday, a new network segment for VoIP traffic was implemented. A separate VLAN for “Voice” was created. Could this be relevant? 24.07.25 09:05 – Internal analysis SBC logs reviewed: – SIP INVITE successful – 200 OK correct – Call established – After 30 seconds, BYE from SBC No obvious error code. 24.07.25 10:30 – Firewall analysis No blocking visible. Ports open according to standard configuration. 24.07.25 14:20 – Detailed SIP analysis Session-Expires header reviewed. Session timer set to 30 seconds. Comparison with other locations shows: There, 180 seconds. 25.07.25 08:50 – Review of internal documentation Document: “Blueprint – Microsoft Teams Direct Routing & Session Handling – Version 2.1” Section on NAT session persistence and SIP session timers relevant. Note in the blueprint: For certain firewall or NAT configurations, the session timer must be adjusted to avoid premature termination. 25.07.25 11:15 – Internal tests Temporary adjustment of session parameters on SBC. Test call performed. Call remains stable for several minutes. Reverting to original configuration → disconnection after approx. 30 seconds reproducible. 28.07.25 – Coordination with network team New “Voice” VLAN uses different NAT policy. UDP session timeout configured to 30 seconds. Comparison with Zurich location → 180 seconds. 29.07.25 – Adjustment NAT session timeout adjusted according to architecture specification. Details not documented in ticket for security reasons. 30.07.25 – Customer feedback Good morning We conducted several test calls. No more disconnections observed. Longer conversations are also stable. Thank you very much. 31.07.25 15:52 – Ticket closure Dear Contact Person 16 The root cause was a deviation in the session and NAT configuration at the Basel location related to the new VLAN structure. The configuration was adjusted according to the internal Direct Routing architecture. Calls now remain stable. The blueprint (authoritative guidance) contains a decision tree for technicians, explicit monitoring recommendations, and enumerated failure modes; AD CS (Root and Issuing CA) is a core backend dependency whose misconfiguration or expiry causes authentication failures or certificate issuance failures. The decision tree explicitly routes unresolved issuance problems to Infrastructure/AD CS specialists as the escalation target. Recommended controls in the blueprint include separating Root and Issuing CA roles, implementing monitoring of certificate requests and errors, validating IIS/service-account/AD CS post-configuration steps, and creating clear escalation/runbook paths for persistent issuance faults.
