It explicitly describes NDES roles, failure modes, and stepwise remediation actions, making it the single consolidated reference for technicians deploying or operating this capability. Because the blueprint bundles decision-tree checks, monitoring guidance, and remediation steps (IIS components, AD CS post-configuration, service account settings, CA/template selection), it should be treated as the authoritative runbook for first- and second-line responders and as the basis for formalizing escalation and change processes for certificate issuance services. [Data: Entities (359); Relationships (579,583,372)] ## Active Directory Certificate Services (AD CS) is a single, high-impact dependency for NDES AD CS provides Root and Issuing CA roles, certificate templates, and lifecycle management and is explicitly described as the backend that NDES depends on; AD CS post-configuration is required for NDES to operate. Failures or misconfigurations in AD CS (expired CA certs, wrong CA selection, misconfigured templates) are identified as causes of authentication failures, connection drops, or inability to issue device certificates. Because AD CS issues and lifecycle-management are upstream of NDES, any operational or security change to the CA hierarchy affects device authentication broadly; therefore AD CS must be treated as a critical service with dedicated monitoring, change control, and escalation ownership. [Data: Entities (361); Relationships (596,600)] ## Decision tree encodes operational checks and prescribes escalation to infrastructure specialists The 'DECISION TREE – NDES INSTALLATION & OPERATION' contains a structured troubleshooting flow (seven verification steps) covering reachability, NDES service status, IIS components, AD CS post-configuration, service account configuration, CA/template selection, and final issuance testing—providing a reproducible first-line diagnostic workflow for technicians. When the decision tree’s checks do not resolve issuance failures, it explicitly directs escalation to Infrastructure/AD CS specialists as the next-level support for deeper PKI, CA, and server remediation; this creates a clear handoff path from NDES operators to specialists. Operationalizing that handoff (contacts, SLA, and triage criteria) will reduce time-to-resolution for persistent certificate issuance problems. [Data: Entities (372,374); Relationships (607,600)] ## Monitoring of certificate requests and issuance errors is recommended and critical to detect indirect service impacts The blueprint contains a dedicated operational guidance item 'MONITORING OF CERTIFICATE REQUESTS AND ERRORS' that recommends tracking issuance requests, errors, expiries, and communication failures between NDES, AD CS, and target systems as a best practice. It notes that certificate problems often manifest indirectly as authentication failures or connection drops, so targeted monitoring is needed to surface these root causes early. Implementing the monitoring guidance will enable proactive alerts for expiring CA or device certificates, repeated template- or issuance-errors, and communication failures between NDES and AD CS—allowing teams to remediate before business-impacting authentication outages occur. [Data: Entities (371); Relationships (583,579)] ## Common failure modes and remediation areas are well-documented and should be converted into testable runbooks The blueprint enumerates common failure modes including expired or invalid certificates, misconfigured templates, disrupted communication paths between NDES and AD CS, missing IIS components, incorrect service account settings, and improper CA/template selection—these are the primary operational causes for certificate issuance failures described in the document. Each listed failure mode maps to concrete verification and remediation steps in the decision tree; converting those steps into short, testable runbooks (with verification checks and telemetry/alert thresholds) will improve first-contact resolution and make escalation to infrastructure specialists more efficient. [Data: Entities (359,372,361)] ## Infrastructure/AD CS specialists are identified as the escalation target but need formalized responsibilities Infrastructure specialists (including AD CS specialists) are identified explicitly as the escalation target when NDES cannot issue certificates after initial troubleshooting; the decision tree and blueprint point to them for deeper PKI and server-level remediation. This clarifies who must be engaged for triage beyond the decision-tree steps. However, the presence of an identified escalation target in the blueprint is not the same as fully operationalized ownership—teams should formalize contact information, SLA expectations, required log/trace artifacts to collect prior to escalation, and regular cross-training sessions so that the escalation path yields consistent outcomes. [Data: Entities (374); Relationships (607,600)] ## Separation of Root and Issuing CA roles is recommended to reduce PKI risk The blueprint and AD CS description recommend clear separation between Root CA and Issuing CA roles to meet best practices and minimize risk to the PKI. This separation reduces blast radius from a compromised issuing CA and supports more secure key custodianship and certificate lifecycle controls. Implementing and documenting a separated CA hierarchy, along with associated access controls, backup/restore procedures, and validation tests, should be treated as a priority security control for the certificate services community. [Data: Entities (361); Relationships (596)] ## Blueprint contains built-in recommendations that enable prioritized remediation actions Because the blueprint already includes a decision tree, monitoring guidance, a list of common failure modes, and escalation targets, it provides sufficient content to prioritize near-term operational improvements: (1) implement monitoring/alerts for issuance-errors and expiry, (2) enforce CA role separation and validate AD CS post-configuration, (3) convert decision-tree checks into formal runbooks and pre-escalation checklists, and (4) formalize escalation SLAs and contact matrices with Infrastructure specialists. These actionable items can be implemented with low‑to‑moderate effort and are directly supported by the blueprint content—making them high‑value first steps to reduce certificate-related outages and security exposures. [Data: Entities (359,371,372,374); Relationships (583,607)]

context: SOURCE 8: ﻿ Ticket summary: Ticket name: KF52: Microsoft Teams – Incoming calls from specific numbers not received Ticket number: TI-1612036 Company: Customer Company 52 AG Contact person: Contact Person 22 Owner: Employee 41 Origin: Email Type: Incident Created on: 18.09.25 08:56 Closure Closure date: 29.09.25 16:18 Closure text: Analysis completed. Routing and translation rules adjusted according to Direct Routing Blueprint. Incoming calls fully functional again. Activities 18.09.25 08:40 – Email from Contact Person 22 Good morning We have noticed that incoming calls from certain customers are no longer reaching us. Callers report that they either receive a busy tone or the connection is disconnected after a few seconds. Other external calls are functioning normally. Could you please check this? Kind regards 18.09.25 08:56 Ticket created and assigned to Employee 41. 18.09.25 09:25 – Helpdesk inquiry Good day Could you please provide the following information: – Example phone numbers where the issue occurs – Since when the issue has existed – Whether all users are affected or only specific ones Thank you. 18.09.25 10:15 – Customer response Here are two examples: +41 44 785 23 10 +41 44 785 23 11 Other numbers such as +41 31 … work without issue. The problem has existed since last week. All employees are affected. 18.09.25 11:40 – Internal analysis Test call from private mobile (different number range) → works. Test with number in specified range → call not delivered. In the Teams Admin Center, no incoming call is shown. 19.09.25 09:05 – Initial hypothesis Possible provider issue. Carrier contacted. 19.09.25 14:20 – Carrier feedback From the carrier’s perspective, the call is correctly delivered to the company’s SBC. No routing errors visible on their side. 22.09.25 08:55 – SBC log analysis For functioning numbers: INVITE accepted and forwarded to Teams. For affected numbers: INVITE received but immediately answered with “404 Not Found”. 22.09.25 11:30 – Email to customer Good day We see that incoming calls from the provider are reaching the SBC. However, the requests are not being correctly forwarded internally. Were there recent changes to number ranges or new extensions? Kind regards 22.09.25 13:05 – Customer response Yes, we received a new number range from the provider last week and partially migrated old numbers. The affected numbers belong to this new range. 23.09.25 09:10 – Internal review Comparison with document “Blueprint – Microsoft Teams Direct Routing & Number Translation – Version 2.3” Section regarding: • Normalization of incoming numbers • E.164 format • Translation rules on SBC • Voice routing policies Finding: New numbers are not included in the SBC translation table. Routing rule covers only the old number range. 24.09.25 – Test Temporary adjustment of translation rule for new number range. Test call performed. Call correctly signaled and delivered. Reverting to original configuration → issue reproducible. 25.09.25 – Coordination with customer Confirmation that new number range was fully migrated. Internal documentation had not yet been updated. 26.09.25 – Adjustment Routing and translation rules extended according to architectural specification. Details not documented in ticket for security reasons. 29.09.25 – Customer feedback All tested numbers are working again. Multiple customers also confirmed successful calls. Thank you for the support. 29.09.25 16:18 – Ticket closure Dear Contact Person 22 The root cause was an inconsistent translation and routing configuration following the introduction of a new number range. The configuration was adjusted according to the internal Direct Routing architecture. Incoming calls are now correctly delivered again. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 74: # SBC Translation Table / Direct Routing — Missing New Number Range This community centers on the PSTN-to-Teams Direct Routing chain: the Carrier/Provider delivers calls to the customer's SBC, the SBC translation table implements the Routing and Translation Rules (including E.164 normalization), and those rules feed Voice Routing Policies that route calls to Teams. During a recent migration the carrier provisioned a New Number Range which was partially migrated by the customer; those new numbers (+41 44 785 23 10/11 are given as examples) were not present in the SBC translation table and associated routing rules, causing INVITEs for that range to be mis-answered (404) and not forwarded to Teams. The carrier confirmed upstream delivery to the SBC, helping isolate the failure to SBC-side translation/routing configuration rather than upstream provisioning. [Data: Entities (177,178,179,180,181,183), Relationships (274,276,278,279,282,275)] ## Primary technical root cause: missing new-range entries in the SBC translation table Investigation identified that new telephone numbers provisioned by the carrier were not present in the SBC translation table, so INVITEs for that range were mis-answered (404) and not forwarded into Teams. This indicates a configuration gap where the concrete SBC translation entries did not include the new number range(s) introduced during migration [Data: Entities (178,183), Relationships (276,282)]. Because the SBC translation table is the concrete implementation of the Routing and Translation Rules, an omission at that configuration artifact directly prevented normalization and mapping for the affected numbers, breaking the call delivery chain to Teams until the entries were added to the table. This is consistent with the relationships tying the translation table to routing rules and the old/new range contrast observed during troubleshooting [Data: Entities (178,179,183), Relationships (276,282)]. ## Carrier confirmation isolates failure to SBC-side processing (not upstream delivery) The carrier/provider reported that they delivered calls correctly to the SBC and saw no routing errors on their side, which helped rule out upstream delivery issues and focus the investigation on SBC processing and configuration [Data: Entities (177), Relationships (274)]. Operationally, this eliminates carrier routing as the immediate cause and shortens the troubleshooting path to SBC translation and normalization logic, which should be the primary focus for immediate remediation and validation steps. The existing carrier confirmation should be recorded in the incident timeline and retained as evidence for post-incident review and change verification [Data: Relationships (274)]. ## Dependency chain: Routing & Translation Rules → E.164 normalization → Voice Routing Policies The components form a linear dependency chain where Routing and Translation Rules implement E.164 normalization and feed normalized numbers into Voice Routing Policies that determine Teams delivery; failures within the translation layer therefore propagate downstream to voice routing and call delivery [Data: Relationships (278,279), Entities (179,181,180)]. This chain highlights a single-point-of-impact: while multiple components participate in call delivery, the translation table acts as the authoritative mapping layer. Any gaps or misconfigurations in the translation table or the translation rules will prevent normalized address generation and thus prevent voice routing policies from matching and delivering calls to Teams users or voicemail/auto-attendants [Data: Entities (178,179,180)]. ## Migration and change-control gap: new range was provisioned but translation rules were not updated The new number range was assigned/provisioned by the carrier and partially migrated by the customer the week prior to the issue, creating a requirement to update SBC translation rules that was not met prior to cutover; the relationship metadata explicitly links the migration to the need to update SBC entries [Data: Entities (183), Relationships (275)]. This sequence suggests a process gap between telecommunications provisioning and SBC configuration change (e.g., missing checklist item, absent automated provisioning, or an incomplete change request). That control gap materially increased the likelihood of service disruption for the new range and should be addressed by process and governance changes (see recommendations). ## Observed failure mode: INVITEs mis-answered with 404 and not forwarded to Teams The direct symptom reported was that INVITEs for numbers in the new range were mis-answered with 404 responses and were not forwarded into Teams, consistent with missing or mismatched normalization/mapping entries on the SBC that prevented routing policies from matching the calls to internal destinations [Data: Entities (178,183), Relationships (276,282)]. This failure mode is reproducible and actionable: adding the correct normalization and translation entries for the new range should return correct call handling and prevent 404 mis-answers for those numbers. As such, verification steps can be scripted into a runbook for validation after updates are applied. ## Immediate remediation priorities and validation steps The highest-priority corrective action is to add the new-number-range entries to the SBC translation table and ensure the corresponding routing/translation rules implement correct E.164 normalization so the Voice Routing Policies match the calls to Teams endpoints [Data: Entities (178,179,181), Relationships (276,279,278)]. Follow-up validation should include (a) inbound test calls from the carrier for affected sample numbers, (b) SBC logs verifying correct translation and normalization, (c) verification that calls reach Teams and that no 404s are returned, and (d) a change-control record that ties the carrier provisioning event to the SBC configuration change to close the loop. Retain the carrier’s confirmation of upstream delivery as supporting evidence [Data: Entities (177,183), Relationships (274,275)]. ## Operational risk and recurrence: process and monitoring deficiencies The root cause and migration timeline point to a repeatable operational risk: when carriers provision new ranges, failure to propagate those ranges into SBC translation rules will repeatedly cause call delivery failures. The presence of an existing correctly functioning old number range makes such omissions less obvious until customers or users on the new range report failures [Data: Entities (184,183), Relationships (282)]. Mitigations should include: a) formalized handoff checklist between carrier provisioning and SBC configuration owners, b) automated reconciliation that compares carrier-provided ranges to SBC translation table contents, c) monitoring/alerting for abnormal 404 rates on the SBC for inbound PSTN INVITEs, and d) test call validation as part of cutover acceptance. ## Stakeholder mapping and escalation pathway Key stakeholders in this community are the Carrier/Provider (responsible for PSTN provisioning and initial delivery), the SBC owner/telephony/networking team (responsible for translation tables and routing rules), and the Teams/UC platform team (consumer of normalized addresses via Voice Routing Policies). The relationships show direct handoffs from Carrier → SBC and SBC configuration → Voice Routing Policies that affect Teams delivery [Data: Entities (177,178,180,179), Relationships (274,278)]. Escalation should follow: (1) carrier confirms delivery (already done) to quickly isolate the issue, (2) SBC/telephony team applies translation updates and notifies Teams/UC team, (3) Teams/UC validates call delivery and closes the loop, and (4) change-control and post-incident review document the migration and corrective steps for governance and audit.
